era_context: The Late Bronze Age collapse is ongoing (c. 1200–1150 BCE). Egypt under Ramesses III is in the midst of military campaigns and economic challenges. The Hittite Empire has collapsed; Anatolia is fragmented. The Shang dynasty in China is in its final phase. In Mesoamerica, the Middle Preclassic period is underway (not the Classic period, which begins c. 250 CE).

events.title: Ramesses III's military campaigns and administrative consolidation
events.description: Ramesses III continues his reign, conducting military campaigns and administrative consolidation. The pharaoh is dealing with multiple threats: the Sea Peoples, internal economic strain, and administrative challenges. The reign of Ramesses III (r. 1186–1155 BCE) represents the last major period of Egyptian military power and cultural achievement, though the underlying economic and political foundations are weakening.

sources.name: Papyrus Harris I and administrative records

events.certainty_note: Ramesses III's reign is well-documented, though specific events in 1152 BCE are not individually attested.

events.title: Late Bronze Age collapse: widespread disruption in Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: The Late Bronze Age collapse is in its final stages or recently concluded. This period (c. 1200–1150 BCE) saw the collapse of multiple major civilisations and empires: the Mycenaean palatial system in Greece, the Hittite Empire in Anatolia, the Ugarit kingdom in the Levant, and the weakening of Egypt. The causes remain debated: climate change (drought), invasions by the Sea Peoples, internal conflicts, and systemic fragility all likely contributed. By 1152 BCE, the collapse is largely complete, and the Eastern Mediterranean is in a period of reduced complexity, population movement, and cultural transformation.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from multiple sites showing destruction layers and abandonment
Egyptian inscriptions mentioning the Sea Peoples

events.certainty_note: The Late Bronze Age collapse is well-established archaeologically and historically. The exact causes and chronology are debated, but the phenomenon itself is secure.
cross_references: Part of broader pattern of civilisational transitions

events.title: Shang dynasty in final decline; Zhou expansion
events.description: The Shang dynasty continues its decline, while the Zhou people expand their power. The transition is imminent (traditionally dated to 1046 BCE). The specific situation in 1152 BCE is not documented in contemporary sources.
key_figures: Di Xin / Zhou Xin (last Shang king, if reigning)
King Wen of Zhou (or his predecessor)
events.certainty_note: Chinese chronology is uncertain by ±30–50 years for this period.

events.title: Aramean tribes expanding in the Levant and Mesopotamia
events.description: Aramean tribes, a Semitic-speaking people, are expanding their presence in the Levant and Mesopotamia during the Late Bronze Age collapse. The Arameans are semi-nomadic pastoralists who gradually settle in urban centres and establish kingdoms. By the Iron Age, Aramean kingdoms such as Aram-Damascus will become significant regional powers. The expansion of the Arameans is part of the broader pattern of population movements and political reorganisation during the Late Bronze Age collapse.
events.certainty_note: The expansion of Aramean tribes is well-established archaeologically and historically, but specific events in 1152 BCE are not documented.
cross_references: Part of broader Late Bronze Age collapse and population movements
Leads to establishment of Aramean kingdoms in Iron Age

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1152 BCE is similar to previous years in this batch: heavily weighted toward the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. The Late Bronze Age collapse is one of the most debated phenomena in ancient history, with scholars disagreeing on causes, chronology, and severity. Egyptian sources are biased toward royal narratives. Chinese chronology remains uncertain. Most other regions lack contemporary written records.

graph_edges.note: Late Bronze Age collapse and Ramesses III's military campaigns are contemporary phenomena
Aramean expansion during Late Bronze Age collapse led to establishment of Aramean kingdoms in Iron Age
